questions.question_text: Under an exclusive right-to-sell listing, the broker earns a commission if the property sells during the listing period:
questions.correct_answer: Regardless of who finds the buyer
distractors: Only if the listing broker or a cooperating broker procures the buyer
Only if the buyer was found through the MLS during the listing
Only if the seller did not know the buyer before signing the listing
questions.explanation: An exclusive right-to-sell listing gives the broker the right to a commission no matter who produces the buyer during the listing period — even if the seller independently finds their own buyer with no agent involvement. This is the most protective listing type for brokers and the most common. It ensures the broker is compensated for their marketing investment regardless of how the buyer is found. The seller who wants to retain the right to sell on their own without paying commission needs an exclusive agency listing or open listing, not an exclusive right-to-sell.

questions.question_text: A seller lists their home under an exclusive agency listing and then sells it themselves to a neighbor without any broker involvement. The seller owes the listing broker:
questions.correct_answer: No commission
distractors: The full commission
Half the commission
The commission only if the broker can prove they also identified the neighbor
questions.explanation: An exclusive agency listing gives one broker the exclusive right to represent the seller (no other brokers may be hired), but the seller retains the right to sell the property themselves without owing a commission. The commission is only earned if the broker or a cooperating broker procures the buyer. This distinguishes it from an exclusive right-to-sell, where the broker earns a commission regardless of who finds the buyer. The exclusive agency listing is less common because brokers invest marketing resources with the risk that the seller's own efforts may result in no commission.

questions.question_text: An open listing differs from exclusive listings in that with an open listing, the seller:
questions.correct_answer: May list with multiple brokers, and only the procuring broker earns a commission
distractors: Must pay all brokers who attempted to sell the property
Cannot list with another broker for 90 days after the listing expires
Cannot sell on their own without paying a commission to all brokers
questions.explanation: An open listing is a non-exclusive arrangement where the seller may simultaneously engage multiple brokers. Only the broker who is the procuring cause of the sale — who first produces the ready, willing, and able buyer — earns a commission. If the seller finds their own buyer, no commission is owed to anyone. Open listings are common in commercial real estate and rare in residential, because most MLS systems require exclusive listing agreements for member brokers to enter a property. Brokers are less motivated to invest in marketing a property when they may get no commission even if they find an interested buyer.

questions.question_text: A net listing is considered ethically problematic primarily because:
questions.correct_answer: The broker keeps all proceeds above the seller's net, creating a conflict
distractors: Net listings violate federal RESPA anti-kickback provisions
The seller cannot determine their net proceeds in advance
Net listings require the broker to guarantee the seller's net amount
questions.explanation: In a net listing, the seller specifies a minimum net amount they want from the sale. The broker keeps everything above that amount as their commission. This creates a serious conflict of interest: the broker is motivated to sell at the highest possible price — not necessarily to serve the seller's best interests — and the seller may not know how much the broker is actually making. Net listings are illegal in several states and strongly discouraged by the NAR Code of Ethics in others. Brokers who use net listings risk discipline for failing to disclose their compensation amount to the seller.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs an exclusive buyer representation agreement with an agent. Before the agreement expires, the buyer purchases a FSBO property without using the agent. The buyer likely:
questions.correct_answer: Owes the agent a commission
distractors: Owes nothing
Owes nothing because FSBO properties are excluded from buyer representation agreements
Owes a reduced commission since the agent did not locate the property
questions.explanation: An exclusive buyer representation agreement obligates the buyer to work exclusively with that agent and, typically, to compensate the agent if they purchase any property during the agreement period — regardless of how the property was found or whether it was listed on the MLS. FSBO properties are not exempt. The buyer agreed to these terms when signing. This is why buyers should understand buyer representation agreements before signing and should negotiate appropriate carve-outs (e.g., specific properties already known to the buyer) before executing. The agreement terms govern whether compensation is owed.

questions.question_text: A property management agreement is MOST similar to which other brokerage agreement?
questions.correct_answer: A listing agreement
distractors: A buyer representation agreement
A referral agreement
A dual agency agreement
questions.explanation: A property management agreement authorizes a broker to manage real property on behalf of the owner — collecting rents, maintaining the property, leasing to tenants, and handling day-to-day operations. Like a listing agreement, it creates an agency relationship where the broker acts as the owner's agent with defined authority, duties, and compensation. Both are governed by the same fiduciary principles. Property managers must hold a real estate license in most states because their activities (leasing, negotiating, collecting rents for others) fall within the definition of real estate brokerage.

questions.question_text: Before the 2024 NAR settlement, a listing broker's offer of compensation to cooperating brokers through the MLS was BEST understood as:
questions.correct_answer: A unilateral offer accepted by performance
distractors: A mandatory fee set by the MLS that all member brokers had to accept
A negotiated split agreed upon between both brokers before listing
A government-regulated commission that could not be changed once published
questions.explanation: Under the pre-2024 MLS model, cooperation worked on a unilateral offer / acceptance basis: the listing broker published a unilateral offer of compensation to any MLS member who procured a buyer, and a cooperating broker accepted that offer by performance — producing a ready, willing, and able buyer — at which point a bilateral co-op agreement was formed between the two brokers. Commissions were never set by the MLS or by any government body; they have always been negotiable. The practice ended with the 2024 NAR settlement, which prohibits offers of compensation from being communicated through the MLS — broker compensation is now handled through written buyer agreements and off-MLS negotiation.

questions.question_text: A seller with an exclusive right-to-sell listing finds their own buyer through a yard sign. The listing broker:
questions.correct_answer: Earns the full commission
distractors: Earns no commission because the broker did not find this buyer
Earns half commission since the seller did some marketing
Must negotiate a reduced fee since broker marketing was not the cause
questions.explanation: The exclusive right-to-sell listing is the most protective for brokers because it guarantees a commission on any sale during the listing period, no matter who finds the buyer. Even if the seller puts up their own yard sign and the buyer walks in without any broker involvement, the commission is still owed. This is why it is the most common listing type — brokers can invest in marketing knowing their compensation is secure. Sellers who want the option to sell on their own without paying commission should consider an exclusive agency or open listing.

questions.question_text: Why do most brokers prefer the exclusive right-to-sell listing over other listing types?
questions.correct_answer: It guarantees commission on any sale during the term, protecting the broker's investment
distractors: It allows the broker to set the listing price without the seller's input
It automatically renews at the end of the term without a new agreement
It permits the broker to represent both parties without additional disclosure
questions.explanation: Brokers invest substantial time and money in marketing a listed property — professional photography, MLS fees, online advertising, open houses, and agent time. The exclusive right-to-sell listing protects this investment by ensuring the broker earns a commission however the buyer is found. Without this protection (as in an open or exclusive agency listing), the broker risks spending thousands in marketing only to have the seller find their own buyer and owe nothing.

questions.question_text: An exclusive right-to-sell listing must include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.correct_answer: A guaranteed minimum sale price the broker must achieve
distractors: A definite expiration date after which the listing terminates
The amount or rate of the broker's commission or compensation
A description of the property being offered for sale
questions.explanation: Listing agreements must include: the parties involved, property description, listing price, commission amount or rate, a definite expiration date, and the broker's authority. They do not guarantee a minimum sale price — the broker's obligation is to use reasonable effort to market and sell the property, not to guarantee a specific result. Many state licensing boards specifically require a definite termination date to prevent open-ended listings that could trap sellers in agreements indefinitely.

questions.question_text: A protection clause (safety clause) in an exclusive right-to-sell listing protects the broker by:
questions.correct_answer: The broker earns a commission if a listed buyer closes after expiration
distractors: The seller cannot list with another broker for six months
The seller must reimburse marketing costs if unsold
The broker is guaranteed rehire if the seller relists
questions.explanation: A protection clause (also called a safety, carryover, or extender clause) protects the broker from losing a commission when a buyer they introduced during the listing period waits until after expiration to purchase. Typically, the clause covers a specified period (30-180 days after expiration) and applies only to buyers the broker can document having shown or introduced. Without this clause, a buyer and seller could simply wait for the listing to expire and then complete the sale without paying any commission.

questions.question_text: The key difference between an exclusive right-to-sell and an exclusive agency listing is:
questions.correct_answer: Under an exclusive agency, the seller can sell on their own without paying commission
distractors: An exclusive agency lets the seller hire multiple brokers simultaneously
An exclusive right-to-sell listing has a shorter duration
Only exclusive right-to-sell listings may be entered into the MLS
questions.explanation: Both listing types grant one broker exclusive representation — no other brokers may be hired. The critical difference is what happens when the seller finds their own buyer: under an exclusive right-to-sell, the commission is owed regardless; under an exclusive agency, the seller who finds their own buyer owes nothing. Both types can be entered in the MLS, and neither type has a mandated duration. The exclusive agency listing is less common because brokers bear more risk.

questions.question_text: Under an exclusive agency listing, a cooperating broker from another firm brings a buyer. The listing broker:
questions.correct_answer: Earns the commission
distractors: Does not earn a commission because the cooperating broker found the buyer
Must split the commission equally with the cooperating broker
Earns commission only if the cooperating broker was authorized by the seller
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive agency listing, the seller's right to sell without paying commission applies only to buyers the seller personally finds. If any broker — the listing broker or a cooperating broker — procures the buyer, the listing broker's commission is earned. The listing broker then typically pays a co-op commission to the cooperating broker per whatever cooperation/compensation agreement exists between the brokers. The seller's exemption is narrow: only their own, personal, broker-free sale efforts.

questions.question_text: A seller who wants to try selling on their own while still having professional broker representation should consider:
questions.correct_answer: An exclusive agency listing
distractors: An exclusive right-to-sell listing
A net listing
An oral listing agreement
questions.explanation: The exclusive agency listing is designed for sellers who want professional marketing and representation but also want to try selling the property themselves. If the seller finds their own buyer, no commission is owed; if the broker or a cooperating broker finds the buyer, the commission is earned. An exclusive right-to-sell would require commission even on a seller-found buyer. Net listings have ethical issues, and oral listing agreements are unenforceable under the statute of frauds in virtually all states.

questions.question_text: Why are exclusive agency listings less common than exclusive right-to-sell listings?
questions.correct_answer: Brokers risk investing in marketing without guaranteed compensation
distractors: Exclusive agency listings are prohibited in most states
Sellers cannot enter exclusive agency listings into the MLS
Exclusive agency listings require a longer term than other types
questions.explanation: Brokers are reluctant to invest in marketing — photography, advertising, open houses, MLS fees — when the seller could sell independently and owe nothing. The exclusive right-to-sell protects the broker's investment by guaranteeing compensation. Most brokers prefer this certainty, so exclusive agency listings are relatively rare in practice. They are legal everywhere and can be entered in the MLS, but brokers usually recommend the exclusive right-to-sell for its greater protection.

questions.question_text: A seller has open listings with three different brokers. Broker B brings a buyer whose offer the seller accepts. The commission is owed to:
questions.correct_answer: Broker B only
distractors: All three brokers equally since each had a valid listing agreement
The broker whose listing was signed first, regardless of who found the buyer
No one
questions.explanation: In an open listing, only the broker who is the procuring cause of the sale — meaning they introduced the buyer whose offer is accepted — earns a commission. The other brokers with open listings earn nothing. This creates a competitive dynamic where each broker races to find a buyer. If the seller finds their own buyer, no commission is owed to any broker. Procuring cause disputes are one reason open listings are less common in residential real estate.

questions.question_text: Procuring cause in real estate refers to:
questions.correct_answer: The broker whose efforts primarily caused the buyer to purchase
distractors: The first broker to show the property, regardless of later involvement
The broker who negotiated the lowest purchase price for the buyer
The seller's listing agent who originally marketed and attracted attention
questions.explanation: Procuring cause is a legal concept determining which broker's efforts were the direct and proximate cause of the sale. It is not simply who showed the property first or who negotiated the price. Factors include: who introduced the buyer to the property, who maintained contact, who negotiated terms, and whose efforts led to the buyer's decision to purchase. Procuring cause disputes are resolved by examining the entire chain of events, not a single action.

questions.question_text: Open listings are more common in which type of real estate?
questions.correct_answer: Commercial real estate
distractors: Single-family residential
New construction
Luxury residential
questions.explanation: Open listings are more practical in commercial real estate where properties may have specialized appeal, and owners benefit from multiple brokers reaching different buyer pools (investors, owner-occupants, developers). In residential real estate, most MLS systems require exclusive listings, making open listings rare. Sellers and brokers both prefer the clarity of exclusive arrangements for homes — exclusive listings provide better marketing commitment and fewer disputes about procuring cause.

questions.question_text: Which listing type creates the LEAST motivation for a broker to invest in marketing the property?
questions.correct_answer: An open listing
distractors: An exclusive right-to-sell
An exclusive agency
A buyer representation agreement
questions.explanation: Open listings provide the least broker motivation because multiple brokers compete for the same commission, and the seller can also sell independently. A broker who spends money on advertising may see another broker (or the seller) complete the sale. Exclusive right-to-sell listings create the most motivation because the broker's commission is guaranteed. Exclusive agency listings fall in between — the broker is protected against other brokers but not against the seller's own efforts.

questions.question_text: A seller tells the broker: 'I want $300,000 net from the sale. You keep everything above that as your fee.' This is:
questions.correct_answer: A net listing
distractors: An exclusive right-to-sell with a fixed commission of $300,000
A standard percentage-based listing on the $300,000 amount
An open listing since the seller set a minimum without a formal contract
questions.explanation: A net listing is defined by how the broker's compensation is calculated: the seller names a net amount they want to receive, and the broker keeps everything above that amount. If the property sells for $350,000, the broker earns $50,000; if it sells for $301,000, the broker earns $1,000. This structure is prohibited in many states because it creates a conflict of interest — the broker is motivated to sell at the highest possible price, potentially at the expense of a quick sale that might better serve the seller.

questions.question_text: In states where net listings are legal, what additional obligation does the broker have?
questions.correct_answer: The broker must disclose the selling price and their compensation to the seller
distractors: The broker must obtain a court order approving the listing before marketing
The broker must cap their commission at a percentage set by the state
The broker must have a second broker review the transaction for fairness
questions.explanation: Even in states that permit net listings, brokers must fully disclose the actual selling price and the amount of their compensation to the seller at or before closing. Without this disclosure, the seller might not realize how much the broker earned. For example, if the seller wants $300,000 net and the property sells for $400,000, the broker earns $100,000 — far more than a standard commission. Full disclosure protects the seller and is required by fiduciary duty and most license laws.

questions.question_text: The main ethical concern with net listings is that:
questions.correct_answer: The broker may prioritize a higher price over the seller's other interests
distractors: The seller must accept any offer above their stated net amount
The broker is forced to market the property for less than market value
The listing agreement cannot include a definite expiration date
questions.explanation: With a net listing, the broker's financial incentive is to sell at the highest possible price since they keep the excess. This can conflict with the seller's interests — for example, the seller might prefer a quick sale at a reasonable price over waiting months for a higher offer. The broker might reject or discourage good offers to hold out for a bigger commission. This misalignment of interests is why net listings are banned or restricted in many states.

questions.question_text: A broker sells a property under a net listing for $500,000. The seller's net was $350,000. The broker earned $150,000 — roughly three times a normal commission. The broker:
questions.correct_answer: Must disclose the full amount
distractors: Has done nothing wrong since the seller agreed to the terms
Is liable for the excess and must refund anything above normal commission
Must donate the excess to the state real estate recovery fund
questions.explanation: Even when the seller agreed to the net listing, the broker still owes fiduciary duties — including full disclosure of compensation. A broker who conceals the size of their commission may face claims of fraud, breach of fiduciary duty, or license discipline. The broker is not automatically required to refund the excess (the seller did agree to the arrangement), but failure to fully and prominently disclose the compensation amount is a serious violation. This scenario illustrates exactly why net listings are controversial.

questions.question_text: A buyer representation agreement typically specifies all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.correct_answer: A guaranteed maximum purchase price the agent must achieve
distractors: The duration and conditions under which it may be terminated
The type of property sought and the geographic search area
The amount or method of the agent's compensation
questions.explanation: Buyer representation agreements define the scope of the relationship: duration, property type, geographic area, compensation terms, and duties. They do not guarantee the agent will find a property at a specific price — that depends on market conditions. The agent's obligation is to use reasonable effort to help the buyer find suitable properties, not to guarantee a particular outcome or price.

questions.question_text: Following the 2024 NAR settlement, buyer representation agreements have become more important because:
questions.correct_answer: Buyers must sign a written agreement before an agent can show MLS-listed properties
distractors: Sellers are now prohibited from offering any compensation to buyer's agents
Buyer agents can no longer receive compensation from any source except the buyer
The settlement eliminated exclusive buyer representation in favor of open agreements
questions.explanation: The 2024 NAR settlement requires MLS-participating buyer's agents to have a written buyer representation agreement in place before touring homes. This ensures buyers understand and agree to the compensation terms upfront. While sellers can still offer buyer agent compensation (just not through the MLS offer of compensation field), the settlement emphasizes transparency about who pays and how much. Buyer agents can still be compensated through various arrangements — including seller concessions negotiated in the purchase offer.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a non-exclusive buyer representation agreement. This means the buyer:
questions.correct_answer: May work with other agents simultaneously without breaching the agreement
distractors: Is committed to purchasing through this agent exclusively
Cannot attend open houses without being accompanied by their agent
Must pay the agent even if another agent finds the purchased property
questions.explanation: A non-exclusive buyer representation agreement allows the buyer to work with multiple agents. The buyer owes commission only to the agent who actually helps them purchase a property. This is the buyer equivalent of an open listing — multiple agents may compete to serve the buyer, and only the one who succeeds earns compensation. Most agents prefer exclusive agreements, as they protect the agent's investment of time in searching, showing properties, and advising the buyer.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent compensation under a buyer representation agreement may be paid by:
questions.correct_answer: The buyer, the seller through a concession, or a combination
distractors: Only the buyer
Only the seller
The MLS
questions.explanation: Buyer agent compensation can come from multiple sources. Traditionally, it was paid through the MLS co-op offer from the seller. Following the 2024 NAR settlement changes, compensation is more commonly addressed through direct buyer-broker agreements, but sellers may still offer concessions that cover buyer agent fees. The buyer representation agreement should clearly state how much the agent will be compensated and that any seller contribution will offset (not add to) the buyer's obligation.

questions.question_text: A property management agreement creates what type of relationship between the owner and the manager?
questions.correct_answer: A general agency
distractors: A special agency
A universal agency
An independent contractor relationship with no fiduciary obligations
questions.explanation: A property management agreement typically creates a general agency relationship. The manager has broad, ongoing authority to act on the owner's behalf within defined parameters — collecting rent, hiring maintenance, screening tenants, handling emergencies. This differs from a special agency (like a listing agent authorized for one transaction) and a universal agency (like a power of attorney that covers everything). The manager owes the owner full fiduciary duties within the scope of the agreement.

questions.question_text: A property manager who collects rent from tenants must deposit those funds:
questions.correct_answer: Into the owner's designated trust account
distractors: Into the manager's personal account until the end of the month
Into any account the manager chooses if monthly reports are provided
Directly to the owner within 24 hours without using an interim account
questions.explanation: Rent collected on behalf of a property owner is trust money and must be handled with the same care as any other client funds. The manager must deposit rents into a trust or escrow account designated in the property management agreement. Depositing into a personal account is commingling — a serious license violation. The manager then disburses funds as authorized: paying expenses, retaining management fees, and remitting the balance to the owner per the agreement's schedule.

questions.question_text: A property manager's fiduciary duties to the property owner include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.correct_answer: Guaranteeing the property will always be occupied and producing income
distractors: Accounting for all funds received and disbursed on the owner's behalf
Disclosing known maintenance issues affecting property value
Acting with loyalty by placing the owner's interests above their own
questions.explanation: Property managers owe owners the same fiduciary duties as any agent: loyalty, disclosure, accounting, confidentiality, obedience, and reasonable care. However, no fiduciary duty guarantees results — the manager cannot promise 100% occupancy or guaranteed income. The manager's obligation is to use reasonable skill and diligence in marketing, screening tenants, and maintaining the property. Vacancies are a normal part of rental property ownership that even the best management cannot entirely eliminate.

questions.question_text: A property management agreement should specify the manager's authority regarding:
questions.correct_answer: Spending limits for repairs, tenant screening criteria, and lease terms
distractors: The manager's personal background and educational qualifications
The maximum number of tenants per unit based on manager preference
The minimum occupancy rate the manager must maintain
questions.explanation: A well-drafted property management agreement clearly defines the manager's scope of authority — how much they can spend on repairs without owner approval, what criteria to use when screening tenants, what lease terms are acceptable, and how to handle emergencies. Without clear authority boundaries, disputes arise about whether the manager exceeded their authority. The agreement should also specify compensation, reporting requirements, and termination procedures.

questions.question_text: The Multiple Listing Service (MLS) primarily benefits sellers by:
questions.correct_answer: Exposing the property to all member brokers and their buyers
distractors: Setting the listing price based on automated valuation models
Guaranteeing the property will sell within the listing period
Eliminating the need for the listing broker to market individually
questions.explanation: The MLS is a cooperative system where listing brokers share property information with all member brokers. This dramatically increases the property's exposure — instead of relying on one broker's buyer pool, the property is visible to every agent in the market. The MLS does not set prices, guarantee sales, or replace individual marketing efforts. It is a distribution channel that multiplies the reach of the listing broker's efforts through broker cooperation.

questions.question_text: Following the 2024 NAR settlement, a cooperating broker who finds a buyer for an MLS-listed property is typically compensated by:
questions.correct_answer: Whatever source the parties' written agreements provide — the buyer directly, seller concessions, or the listing broker off-MLS
distractors: Always the listing broker via the MLS compensation field
Always the buyer in cash at closing
The MLS itself from membership dues
questions.explanation: Since the 2024 NAR settlement, offers of compensation can no longer be communicated through the MLS. A cooperating (buyer's) broker's compensation now comes from whatever the transaction's written agreements provide: the buyer may pay their broker directly under a written buyer representation agreement, the seller may agree to a concession that covers the fee, or the listing broker may still agree to share compensation — but any such sharing must be negotiated off-MLS. There is no longer a single default payor. The MLS itself never pays brokers, and buyers are not automatically responsible in cash at closing.

questions.question_text: Real estate commissions are:
questions.correct_answer: Always negotiable between the broker and their client
distractors: Set at a standard 6% by the National Association of Realtors
Regulated by each state's commission with maximum and minimum rates
Determined by the MLS based on property type and listing price
questions.explanation: Real estate commissions are freely negotiable. There is no standard rate set by law, the MLS, NAR, or any other body. Any agreement among brokers to fix commission rates would violate federal antitrust laws (the Sherman Antitrust Act). Commission rates vary by market, property type, service level, and individual negotiation between the broker and client. Agents who tell clients that commissions are 'standard' or 'set' are making a misrepresentation.

questions.question_text: A procuring cause dispute between two cooperating brokers is MOST commonly resolved through:
questions.correct_answer: Arbitration by the local real estate board or association
distractors: A ruling by the state real estate commission on licensing grounds
Automatic awarding to the broker who first showed the property
Splitting the commission equally regardless of contributions
questions.explanation: MLS rules typically require member brokers to submit procuring cause disputes to arbitration through the local board of Realtors. This process examines the full chain of events — who introduced the buyer, maintained contact, negotiated, and whose efforts led to the sale. The arbitration panel evaluates multiple factors rather than applying a simple rule like 'first to show wins.' State real estate commissions generally do not resolve commission disputes between brokers — they handle license law violations.
